title: 顺水推舟
summary:
Hercule Poirot enters an empty hall at an inn and observes the quiet atmosphere of the guest area. He visits room number five, which has been recently cleaned and polished, though it is missing its usual Axminster rug. While examining the room's furnishings, Poirot inspects a marble fireplace; he discovers that the right side of the fireplace surround is blackened, while the left side remains clean. He also notes that a window in the room overlooks a garage roof and a narrow alley that serves as a discreet shortcut.

Seeking warmth, Poirot returns to a "guest-only" room, where he encounters a formidable, elderly woman with iron-gray hair. The woman expresses xenophobic views, criticizing foreigners, the quality of the inn's food, and the changing social morals of young women. She complains about the presence of soldiers and pilots, as well as a perceived lack of modesty in women's clothing.

The conversation shifts when the woman reveals that she witnessed a woman wearing an orange scarf and makeup exiting room number five late at night, after ten o'clock. The woman claims she saw this individual laughing and chatting with a man inside the room. This event occurred the night before the man was murdered. Expressing her disapproval of the woman's behavior, the elderly witness mentions that she has already complained to Miss Lippincott regarding such indiscretions.
